Case Information: A man presented to the emergency department (ED) on transfer from an outside hospital after falling with an e-cigarette in his mouth several hours earlier. The patient did not remember the exact cause of the fall, but believed he may have lost consciousness.
Physical Examination: Physical examination in the ED showed diffuse supraglottic edema that was most severe in the epiglottis, arytenoids, aryepiglottic folds, and obstructed a view of the true vocal cords. The patient presented with stridor, increased work of breathing, and supplement oxygen requirement.
Diagnostic Tests: - Imaging Studies:    1. Computed tomographic scan showed glottic and supraglottic swelling with no evidence of fractures.    2. Modified barium swallow (at 2-month follow-up)       Image Title: Esophageal Stricture       Image Description: Modified barium swallow showing esophageal stricture.    - Endoscopic Findings:      1. Direct laryngoscopy showed diffuse edema and sloughing of pharyngeal and supraglottic mucosa, most severe in the epiglottis         Image Title: Clinical Image of False Vocal Cords         Image Description: Image of the false vocal cords and aryepiglottic folds taken during tracheostomy      2. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy revealed a circumferential esophageal scar in the distal esophagus and considerable ulceration at the distal antrum and pylorus
Final Diagnosis: Chemical burns of the pharynx and esophagus due to e-cigarette injury
Options: {'A': 'Chemical or thermal burn injury to the oropharynx and larynx from the e-cigarette', 'B': 'Caustic esophageal injury leading to stricture', 'C': 'Chemical burns of the pharynx and esophagus due to e-cigarette injury', 'D': 'Caustic esophageal injury with stricture'}

Case Information: A man in his 40s presented with a 3-month history of discrete, dark red, circular lesions in his right axilla. He endorsed severe pruritus at these sites that would wake him up at night. He denied any recent trauma or history of similar lesions. He denied associated pain, blisters, or systemic symptoms (eg, fever, chills). His medical history was unremarkable, and he noted no environmental triggers or new medications. Treatment with over-the-counter hydrocortisone cream, ketoconazole cream, and discontinuation of underarm deodorant yielded no improvement.
Physical Examination: Physical examination revealed 3 well-circumscribed, violaceous, annular patches without scale or erosion that are limited to his right axilla. No other lesions were noted on his skin or mucous membranes. A Wood lamp examination was negative.
Diagnostic Tests: - Biopsy findings:  Image Title: Figure B - Hematoxylin-eosin stain  Image Description: Original magnification ×40 demonstrates lymphocytes obscuring the dermal-epidermal junction    Image Title: Figure C - Hematoxylin-eosin stain  Image Description: Original magnification ×200    - Clinical Photography:  Image Title: Figure A - Clinical photograph  Image Description: Shows oval and annular thin violaceous patches in the right axilla
Final Diagnosis: Inverse lichen planus
Options: {'A': 'Lichen planus pigmentosus', 'B': 'Inverse lichen planus', 'C': 'Lichenoid drug eruption', 'D': 'Discoid lupus erythematosus'}

Case Information: A patient in their 80s presented with dyspnea and syncope during exercise. Medical history included essential hypertension and Parkinson disease. The patient reported experiencing dyspnea on exertion and exercise intolerance for the past year, with difficulty walking more than 100m. They also experienced syncope during a bicycle ride with loss of consciousness for a few minutes, followed by complete recovery.
Physical Examination: Vital signs were normal except for elevated blood pressure of 150/78 mm Hg. Chest auscultation revealed normal breath and heart sounds without any murmurs.
Diagnostic Tests: - Laboratory tests: Hemogram, serum electrolytes, and thyroid function test were within normal limits.- Echocardiogram findings:  - Normal left ventricular ejection fraction (74%)  - Normal wall motion  - Left atrial enlargement (volume index, 34.89 mL/m2)  - Uncertain diastolic function  - No significant valvular abnormality  - Pulmonary hypertension (mean pulmonary arterial pressure 32 mm Hg)- ECG findings:  Image Title: Initial ECG  Image Description: Shows sinus rhythm with first-degree atrioventricular block (PR interval 360 ms), right bundle-branch block (RBBB) and left anterior fascicular block (LAFB). Wide QRS complex (120 ms), rSR in lead V1, and RS in lead V6 (deep and wide S waves ≥40 ms). Left axis deviation and qR in leads I and aVL, and rS in leads II, III, and aVF.- 24-hour ECG monitoring:  Image Title: Continuous ambulatory ECG monitoring  Image Description: Shows sinus rhythm (average 60 beats/min), PR prolongation (360 ms) and third-degree AV block with minimum heart rate of 36 beats/min. Mobitz type I second-degree AV block with pause and junctional escape beat observed. No atrial/ventricular arrhythmia found.
Final Diagnosis: Trifascicular heart block (Bifascicular block with first-degree AV block progressing to complete heart block)
Options: {'A': 'Age-related cardiac conduction disease', 'B': 'Complete heart block (Third-degree AV block)', 'C': 'Third-degree atrioventricular block', 'D': 'Trifascicular heart block (Bifascicular block with first-degree AV block progressing to complete heart block)'}

Case Information: A 36-year-old male patient with no cigarette smoking history or sedentary lifestyle (BMI: 24.2), with no special medical history, presented with a 6-hour history of dizziness, vomiting and unstable walking. The patient had been in good health, with no traditional risk factors such as hypertension, diabetes, or coronary heart disease. No family history of stroke was reported. The patient mentioned having a large rotation range of the head and neck before onset.
Physical Examination: Physical findings:- Patient was conscious with clear speech- Left finger nose test and heel knee tibia test were slightly inaccurate- Romberg sign was positive- NIHSS (National Institute of Health stroke scale) score: 1 point- Cerebrospinal fluid pressure: 270mmH2O- Cerebrospinal fluid protein: 53mg/dl
Diagnostic Tests: 1. Imaging Studies:- Emergency brain CT: No hemorrhage found- Brain MR: Multiple irregular high signal hyperintensity in left thalamus, left occipital lobe and left cerebellum- Brain MR enhancement (7 days after onset): Multiple subacute lacunar infarction in left thalamus, left occipital lobe and left cerebellar hemisphere- Head and neck CTA: Fenestration deformity of V2–V3 segment of left vertebral artery, duplication shape type2. Laboratory Tests:- Blood routine: No increase in eosinophils- Negative results for: MPO-ANCA, PR3-ANCA, GBM antibodies- Normal results for: Blood lipid, blood glucose, rheumatism, autoimmune index, homocysteine, hepatitis B, hepatitis C, syphilis, HIV- CSF: Normal red blood cell and white blood cell counts- Negative results for various antibodies in CSF and serum3. Other Tests:- 24h-dynamic-electrocardiogram: No atrial fibrillation or other arrhythmias- Transcranial Doppler foam test: No abnormal embolism- Cardiac color ultrasound: No obvious abnormality
Final Diagnosis: Acute cerebral infarction of posterior circulation associated with vertebral artery fenestration deformity
Options: {'A': 'Acute cerebral infarction of posterior circulation associated with vertebral artery fenestration deformity', 'B': 'Left vertebral artery dissection leading to posterior circulation ischemic stroke', 'C': 'Thrombosis in a fenestrated vertebral artery', 'D': 'Vertebrobasilar insufficiency secondary to vertebral artery fenestration'}

Case Information: A 27-year-old woman was introduced to the department with elevated hepatobiliary enzymes. The patient occasionally drank alcohol. She had no history of viral hepatitis, steatohepatitis, autoimmune liver disease, or metabolic liver disease. The patient was taking axitinib, azelnidipine, and tramadol hydrochloride. After stopping alcohol and axitinib, her hepatobiliary enzyme levels remained unchanged.
Physical Examination: No specific physical examination findings were reported in the case.
Diagnostic Tests: - Laboratory Tests:    * Aspartate aminotransferase: 55 U/L (reference: 13-30 U/L)    * Alanine aminotransferase: 33 U/L (reference: 7-23 U/L)    * Lactate dehydrogenase: 326 U/L (reference: 124-222 U/L)    * Gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase: 257 U/L (reference: 9-32 U/L)    * Alkaline phosphatase: 1028 U/L (reference: 106-322 U/L)- Imaging Studies:    * Abdominal Ultrasonography      Title: Ultrasonographic findings of hepatic parenchyma      Description: Coarse and heterogeneous relatively hyperechoic hepatic parenchyma as a whole and an irregular isoechoic region from the hepatic hilum to the right dorsal side    * Contrast-enhanced CT      Title: CT findings in arterial phase      Description: Enhancement in the liver except for the region corresponding to the isoechoic region    * Gadolinium-enhanced MRI      Title: MRI findings in hepatobiliary phase      Description: Region corresponding to relatively hyperechoic hepatic parenchyma was clearly hypointense compared with hyperintensity in the region corresponding to the isoechoic region- Pathological Findings:    * Liver Biopsy (hyperechoic parenchyma):      - Scattered clusters of malignant cells positive for PAX8      - Malignant cells in Glisson's capsule, occluding interlobular vein      - Inflammatory cells infiltrated into portal canal with destroyed interlobular bile ducts    * Liver Biopsy (isoechoic region):      - Rare malignant cells      - Slight inflammatory change
Final Diagnosis: Diffuse type of liver metastasis from renal cell carcinoma
Options: {'A': 'Diffuse type of liver metastasis from renal cell carcinoma', 'B': 'Metastatic Renal Cell Carcinoma', 'C': 'Metastatic Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma', 'D': 'Metastatic carcinoma'}

Case Information: A 78-year-old man presented with painful swelling and ulceration of the glans penis of 1 month's duration. His medical history included an elevated white blood cell count and inguinal lymphadenopathy discovered 9 years prior. Four years after initial presentation, he experienced fatigue, weight loss, and loss of appetite. The current illness began 1 month prior to admission when he noticed asymptomatic 'green-yellow welts' and swelling of the glans penis. The swelling became progressively painful and evolved to the size of an 'orange' over 4 days. Previous treatments with topical antibiotics, antifungal agents, hydrogen peroxide soaks, warm compresses, and cephalexin showed no improvement.
Physical Examination: Patient appeared healthy. The glans penis and corona were grossly edematous, with a 3.7 × 3.3-cm tender, firm ulcer on the ventral surface of the penis with overlying black eschar, adherent yellow crust, and granulation tissue. No urethral discharge was present.
Diagnostic Tests:
- Laboratory Tests: Routine blood test results were within normal limits. Cultures, assays, and blood tests evaluating for viral, bacterial, or fungal etiology were negative.

- Pathology Studies: Biopsy of the glans penis ulcer showed dermal infiltrate of atypical lymphocytes, which were CD20+, CD79a+, and CD5+, within the background of a reactive T-cell infiltrate. PCR analysis revealed a clonal IgH gene rearrangement.

- Imaging Studies: PET-CT scan demonstrated FDG-avid abdominal and peripancreatic lymph nodes.
  Image Title: Clinical Appearance of the Penis
  Image Description: Grossly edematous glans penis and corona with a tender, firm ulcer on the ventral surface of the penis with overlying black eschar, adherent yellow crust, and granulation tissue.

  Image Title: Histologic Evaluation of Penile Lesion
  Image Description: Hematoxylin-eosin staining showing dermal infiltrate of large, atypical lymphocytes within a reactive T-cell infiltrate (original magnification ×100)
Final Diagnosis: Richter's transformation presenting as penile ulcer (transformation of chronic lymphocytic leukemia to diffuse large B-cell lymphoma)
Options: {'A': 'Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia/Small Lymphocytic Lymphoma (CLL/SLL)', 'B': 'Diffuse Large B-cell Lymphoma (DLBCL)', 'C': 'CD5-positive diffuse large B-cell lymphoma', 'D': "Richter's transformation presenting as penile ulcer (transformation of chronic lymphocytic leukemia to diffuse large B-cell lymphoma)"}

Case Information: A woman in her 70s presented to her general physician with impacted cerumen in the right ear. Two attempts at bilateral irrigation were made by a practice nurse. The patient reported pain and hearing loss in the left ear immediately after syringing. She subsequently developed purulent and blood-stained discharge in that ear.
Physical Examination: Examination by an ENT specialist found severe otitis externa, with inflammation of the external auditory canal precluding a view of the tympanic membrane. After removal of the wick, the tympanic membrane was visualized and found to have a small anteroinferior perforation. Patient developed a left facial palsy, with lagophthalmos, good static tone of the mouth, but weak movement on smiling (graded as House-Brackman V).
Diagnostic Tests: - Imaging Studies:    CT scan of temporal bones:    Title: Axial Computed Tomographic Scan of Temporal Bone 1 Day After Aural Irrigation    Description: Shows fluid in the left middle ear and mastoid air cells, without evidence of bone erosion. Normal external auditory canal.  - Audiological Tests:    Title: Audiogram and Tympanogram 3 Years After Aural Irrigation    Description: Shows mixed sensorineural and conductive hearing loss in the left ear. Tympanometry in the left ear confirms a high volume owing to tympanic membrane perforation.
Final Diagnosis: Permanent facial palsy and mixed hearing loss secondary to aural irrigation complications
Options: {'A': 'Traumatic tympanic membrane perforation with secondary bacterial otitis media and facial neuritis', 'B': 'Permanent facial palsy and mixed hearing loss secondary to aural irrigation complications', 'C': 'Bell’s palsy (facial nerve palsy of uncertain etiology)', 'D': 'Malignant otitis externa'}

Case Information: A woman in her 60s presented with a new pruritic rash. The rash initially developed on the patient's legs 13 days before presentation and subsequently progressed to her feet, arms, hands, and trunk. Associated symptoms included a burning sensation and occasional pain. Her medical history was notable for endometrial adenocarcinoma status post hysterectomy and recent postoperative radiotherapy (total of 50.4 Gy in 28 fractioned doses), which was completed 4 days after the onset of the rash. She denied any new medications or exposure to chemotherapy, imaging with intravenous contrast, or recent travel. The remainder of her review of systems was unremarkable.
Physical Examination: There were numerous erythematous edematous coalescing papules and plaques on the upper and lower extremities and scattered erythematous papules and plaques on the back and buttocks. Some plaques were annular and polycyclic in configuration, with central hyperpigmentation. Her chest, abdomen, face, and oral mucosa were clear.
Diagnostic Tests:
- Laboratory tests:
  * Complete blood cell count showed eosinophilia of 11%, with an absolute cell count of 0.80 ×103/uL
  * Comprehensive metabolic panel was normal

- Histopathology:
  * Punch biopsy from right thigh showed a moderately dense perivascular and interstitial infiltrate of mononuclear cells with innumerable eosinophils without leukocytoclasia or erythrocyte extravasation

- Images:
  * Image A Title: Clinical photographs of bilateral upper and lower extremities
  * Image A Description: Numerous erythematous edematous coalescing papules and plaques
  * Image B Title: Close-up clinical photograph
  * Image B Description: Plaques with annular and polycyclic configuration with central hyperpigmentation
  * Image C and D Title: Punch biopsy histopathology
  * Image C and D Description: Moderately dense perivascular and interstitial infiltrate of mononuclear cells with innumerable eosinophils (hematoxylin-eosin stain)
Final Diagnosis: Eosinophilic, polymorphic, and pruritic eruption associated with radiotherapy (EPPER)
Options: {'A': 'Eosinophilic, polymorphic, and pruritic eruption associated with radiotherapy (EPPER)', 'B': 'Radiation-induced Wells syndrome', 'C': 'Radiation-associated dermatitis', 'D': 'Eosinophilic dermatitis'}

Case Information: A patient in their 70s presented with dyspnea and fatigue. No history of structural heart disease or hypertension. Medical history significant for diffuse large B-cell lymphoma diagnosed 4 years ago, treated with 8 courses of chemotherapy.
Physical Examination: Blood pressure: 96/54 mm HgHeart rate: 56 bpm
Diagnostic Tests: - Laboratory Tests:  * Serum troponin I: 0.04 ng/mL (normal value, <0.02 ng/mL)  * NT-proBNP: 3896.83 pg/mL (normal value, <100 pg/mL)- ECG Studies:  Image Title: Findings From Past and Present 12-Lead Electrocardiograms (ECGs)  Image Description: Present ECG shows prolonged P-wave duration (180 milliseconds), positive-negative biphasic P wave in leads II, III, and aVF, with shortened or disappeared PR segment. Compared with ECG from 4 years ago.- Imaging Studies:  * Transthoracic echocardiogram: Revealed diffuse medium masses in the right atrium, measuring approximately 50 mm × 22 mm  * PET-CT scan: Showed significantly increased metabolic activity along the right atrial myocardium and adjacent pericardium
Final Diagnosis: Secondary cardiac lymphoma

Case Information: A man in his 40s with a medical history of factor VII deficiency hemophilia presented with acute hypoxic respiratory failure. Following admission, he was intubated with prone positioning, and treatment was initiated. He was also found to have Staphylococcus hemolyticus bacteremia. Three days after admission, the patient developed erythema and edematous necrotic plaques of the bilateral ears. No devices had been used on the ears. The necrosis progressed during the following days, primarily involving the earlobe of the right ear and a large portion of the helix of the left ear.
Physical Examination: Erythematous plaque (2 cm) with overlying eschar on the right earlobe. Multiple erythematous edematous erosions with eschar and crust on the helical rim of the left ear.
Diagnostic Tests: - Biopsy findings:    Image Title: Hematoxylin-eosin−stained biopsy specimens    Image Description: Detached epidermis with full-thickness necrosis overlying sparse inflammation associated with necrosis of adnexal epithelia, including folliculosebaceous units and eccrine glands. Epidermal and dermal necrosis and ulceration with necrosis of the eccrine glands.
Final Diagnosis: Pressure necrosis
Options: {'A': 'Necrotizing fasciitis', 'B': 'Septic emboli with skin necrosis', 'C': 'Necrotizing cellulitis', 'D': 'Pressure necrosis'}

Case Information: A 34-year-old man with a history of asthma presented with chest pain. He had pleuritic and positional chest pain five days prior without infectious symptoms. Initial testing at a rural emergency department was negative and he was discharged. He returned with worsening chest pain and vomiting.
Physical Examination: Initial presentation: Ill-appearing man with no evidence of volume overload or pericardial friction rub. Blood pressure 100/68 mmHg with tachycardia. 24 hours later: Patient was somnolent, tachycardic to 143 beats/min, hypotensive to 77/49 mmHg. Examination showed diaphoresis, elevated jugular venous distension, and pulsus paradoxus.
Diagnostic Tests: - ECG: Sinus tachycardia with diffuse PR-segment depression and ST-segment elevation- Laboratory Tests:  - Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR 58 mm/h, CRP 95.7 mg/L)  - Negative troponins  - Pericardial fluid: 8477 cells (94% polymorphonuclear cells), negative cultures  - Fluid cytology: Abundant neutrophils with scattered eosinophils  - Later tests showed hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, elevated renin with undetectable aldosterone  - Elevated TSH with normal free T4  - Positive for thyroid peroxidase antibodies, 21-hydroxylase and celiac HLA-DQ8 antibodies- Imaging Studies:  Figure 1 - CT Pulmonary Angiogram  Description: Thickened pericardium with small pericardial effusion, no pulmonary embolism  Figure 3 - PET-CT  Description: Patchy pericardial, diffuse thyroid, and bone marrow FDG uptake, without lymph node uptake  Figure 4 - Cardiac MRI  Description: Increased pericardial thickness (4mm) with diffuse circumferential late gadolinium enhancement, pericardial edema on T2-weighted images, pericardial adhesions and subtle respirophasic septal shift- Echocardiogram Findings:  Initial: Moderate-sized circumferential pericardial effusion  Follow-up: Preserved biventricular function, small circumferential pericardial effusion, early diastolic RV collapse, plethoric IVC  Later: LVEF deteriorated to 40% with moderately reduced RV function
Final Diagnosis: Autoimmune Polyglandular Syndrome 2 (APS2)
Options: {'A': 'Autoimmune Pericarditis (Possible Rheumatologic or Endocrine Association)', 'B': 'Addison’s Disease (Primary Adrenal Insufficiency)', 'C': 'Autoimmune Polyglandular Syndrome 2 (APS2)', 'D': "Addison's disease"}

Case Information: A 79-year-old female patient presented with severe chest pain lasting for 4 hours. Medical history included hypertension (moderately managed), paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (treated with Betaloc for 6 months), and moderate pulmonary hypertension (pulmonary artery systolic pressure ~55 mmHg). The patient had no history of diabetes or hyperlipidemia.
Physical Examination: Initial vital signs during procedure showed low blood pressure of 60/40 mmHg, which improved to 90/50 mmHg with heart rate of 96 beats/min after intervention. About 30 minutes post-procedure, patient experienced severe chest pain radiating to the back, with dyspnea and sweating. Vital signs showed blood pressure 100-111/54-70 mmHg, SpO2 84%, and heart rate 110-142 beats/min.
Diagnostic Tests:
- Laboratory Tests:
  • cTnT: 0.309 ng/ml
  • CK-MB: 55 U/L
  • D-Dimer: within normal range

- ECG Findings:
  • Initial ECG showed ST-segment elevation in inferior-posterior leads
  • Post-procedure ECG showed mild ST elevation in leads II, III and AVF; mild ST depression in leads V2 and V4

- Imaging Studies:
  1. Coronary Angiography:
     Title: Emergency Coronary Angiography
     Description: Revealed occlusive thrombosis of the tiny distal left circumflex artery (LCx), with no notable lesions in remaining coronary vessels

  2. Computed Tomography Angiography (CTA):
     Title: Thoracic and Abdominal Aorta CTA
     Description: Revealed pericardial effusion and contrast leakage from myocardium to pericardial cavity

  3. Bedside Echocardiography:
     Description: Showed fluid accumulation within pericardial cavity (10mm anterior to right ventricular anterior wall, 11mm posterior to left ventricular posterior wall, 15mm lateral to left ventricular lateral wall, and 15mm at apex)
Final Diagnosis: Left ventricular free wall rupture following acute myocardial infarction
Options: {'A': 'Ventricular pseudoaneurysm', 'B': 'Myocardial rupture with hemopericardium', 'C': 'Acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI)', 'D': 'Left ventricular free wall rupture following acute myocardial infarction'}

Case Information: A 53-year-old man with chronic hepatitis B virus infection (>30 years) presented with weakness, bloating, anorexia, and decreased urine output for 10 days. He denied any history of infectious disease, such as typhoid or tuberculosis, chronic disease, such as hypertension or diabetes, or recent surgery or trauma.
Physical Examination: Blood pressure on admission was 153/89 mmHg. On July 15, patient developed fever (39°C) with obvious tenderness of the left lumbar vertebra. By July 16, temperature increased to 40.2°C, accompanied by chills, severe low back pain, and forced position.
Diagnostic Tests: - Laboratory tests (on admission):    - ALT: 160 U/L (ref: 0-40)    - AST: 167 U/L (ref: 0-40)    - Serum albumin: 28 g/L (ref: 40-60)    - Total bilirubin: 77.4 μmol/L (ref: 5.1-19)    - Direct bilirubin: 22.4 μmol/L (ref: 0-6)    - WBC: 3.51 × 109/L (ref: 3.5-8.5)    - Neutrophils: 58.1% (ref: 55%-70%)    - Lymphocytes: 22.25% (ref: 20%-40%)    - Hemoglobin: 112 g/L (ref: 120-165)    - Platelets: 74 × 109/L (ref: 125-350)    - Ascites cell count: 200/μL  - Imaging studies:    - Abdominal ultrasound showed liver cirrhosis and ascites with maximum anteroposterior diameter of 132 mm      - Microbiology:    - Blood culture positive with grayish white colonies on blood agar and green colonies on TCBS agar    - 16S rRNA sequencing confirmed organism identity    - Antibiotic susceptibility testing showed sensitivity to multiple antibiotics
Final Diagnosis: Vibrio vulnificus septicemia with underlying hepatitis B virus-associated cirrhosis
Options: {'A': 'Vibrio vulnificus bacteremia', 'B': 'Acute-on-chronic liver failure (decompensated cirrhosis)', 'C': 'Vertebral osteomyelitis (spondylitis) due to Vibrio infection (likely Vibrio parahaemolyticus or Vibrio vulnificus)', 'D': 'Vibrio vulnificus septicemia with underlying hepatitis B virus-associated cirrhosis'}

Case Information: A man in his 40s presented with a 2-year history of multiple asymptomatic lesions on the face and neck. The lesions were round, hypopigmented macules and were symmetrically distributed in the affected area. The macules had appeared on his cheeks 2 years earlier and had gradually coalesced. The size of the lesions ranged from approximately 2 to 3 mm to 25 mm in diameter. There was no hair growth at the site of the lesions, and the patient reported no symptoms. He was previously treated for tinea versicolor, without success.
Physical Examination: Multiple round, hypopigmented macules symmetrically distributed on face and neck. Lesions ranged from 2-3 mm to 25 mm in diameter. Absence of hair growth in affected areas.
Diagnostic Tests: - Skin Biopsy Results:    - Histopathologic findings showed plate-like subepidermal tumor extending horizontally under epidermis with multiple cord-like connections to overlying epidermis    - Neoplastic cords consisted of clear/pale epithelial cells with cytoplasm showing strong periodic acid-Schiff staining due to glycogen accumulation    - Stroma formed by fibrous connective tissue    - Marked elastosis observed in dermis surrounding tumor  Images:    Image A Title: Hypopigmented macules on the face and neck    Image B Title: Absence of hair growth in the affected area    Image C Title: Hematoxylin-eosin staining (original magnification ×10)    Image D Title: Hematoxylin-eosin staining (original magnification ×20)
Final Diagnosis: Tumors of the follicular infundibulum (TFI)
Options: {'A': 'Tumors of the follicular infundibulum (TFI)', 'B': 'Clear cell hidradenoma', 'C': 'Clear cell (pale cell) basal cell carcinoma', 'D': 'Trichoepithelioma'}

Case Information: A patient in their 60s presented to the hospital with sudden loss of consciousness and foaming at the mouth. The patient had no history of arrhythmias. There was no family history of sudden unexpected death or fatal cerebrovascular accident. The patient's spouse stated that the patient had been complaining of chest discomfort for the past 1 month for which the patient was taking Chinese medicine. The patient had drunk about 180 mL of Chinese herbal medicine 20 minutes before developing syncope. The patient's vomit had a pale yellow color.
Physical Examination: Temperature was 36°C, heart rate was 124 beats/min, and blood pressure was 55/32 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry showed arterial oxygen saturation of 99% on room air. Both pupils were 3 mm in diameter and reactive to light. The patient's skin was cool, moist, and cyanosed. Lung fields were clear and heart sounds could not be distinguished due to tachycardia.
Diagnostic Tests:
- Laboratory tests:
  - Troponin T: 6.06 ng/mL (normal range, ≤0.02 ng/mL)
  - Brain-type natriuretic peptide: 2542 pg/mL (normal range, <400 pg/mL)
  - D-dimer: 0.51 µg/mL (normal range, 0-0.55 µg/mL)
  - Qualitative toxicology: positive for aconitine in blood

- ECG findings:
  Image Title: ECG on admission
  Image Description: Regular left deviation of electric axis and right deviation of electric axis alternating of the 2 morphologies of QRS complex tachycardia, retrograde ventriculoatrial conduction in lead V1, atrioventricular dissociation, and beat-to-beat alternation in the ventricular axis

  Image Title: Subsequent ECG
  Image Description: Sinus rhythm with inverted T waves in leads V3 through V6 and Q waves in leads V1 through V4
Final Diagnosis: Bidirectional ventricular tachycardia due to aconitine poisoning
Options: {'A': 'Aconitine poisoning-induced ventricular tachycardia with cardiogenic shock', 'B': 'Malignant ventricular arrhythmia (e.g., bidirectional ventricular tachycardia)', 'C': 'Aconite poisoning (aconitine-induced cardiotoxicity)', 'D': 'Bidirectional ventricular tachycardia due to aconitine poisoning'}

Case Information: A 44-year-old man presented with a 2-year history of headache, dizziness, and imbalance. He had a history of hypertension and cervical disc herniation. One year ago, he underwent transcatheter closure for patent foramen ovale. His father had progressive cognitive decline and imbalance since the age of 50 years and died of cerebral hemorrhage at the age of 65 years.
Physical Examination: Neurological examination revealed brisk bilateral tendon reflexes and bilateral Babinski sign.
Diagnostic Tests: - Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI):    Image 1 Title: Axial Magnetic Resonance Imaging of the Brain    Image 1 Description: T2-weighted fluid-attenuated inversion recovery showing lacunar infarctions and periventricular white matter hyperintensities (WMH)    Image 2 Title: Susceptibility-Weighted Imaging    Image 2 Description: Showing a lobe-dominant distribution of multiple cerebral microbleeds (CMBs) and relatively fewer CMBs in deep regions, mainly involving bilateral parieto-occipital lobes  - Genetic Analysis:    Revealed a heterozygous mutation (c.754G>A, p.A252T) in the HTRA1 gene
Final Diagnosis: Heterozygous HTRA1-related cerebral small vessel disease (CSVD)
Options: {'A': 'HTRA1-related autosomal dominant cerebral small vessel disease', 'B': 'Hypertensive Small Vessel Disease', 'C': 'Hereditary Cerebral Small Vessel Disease (HTRA1-related)', 'D': 'Heterozygous HTRA1-related cerebral small vessel disease (CSVD)'}

Case Information: A female teenage high school student presented with fatigue, recurrent sinus infections, migraines, and anemia. She had one prior episode of immune thrombocytopenic purpura (which required intravenous immunoglobulin treatment), with negative autoimmune workup results. She experienced episodic bouts of abdominal pain since childhood, initially diagnosed as irritable bowel syndrome, with recent colonoscopy results demonstrating collagenous colitis. Case Information: A 9-year-old Chinese girl presenting with symptoms since birth. The patient has thickened nails, keratinized plaques on palms, soles, and corners of the mouth. At age 1, her fingernails and toenails began to turn yellow and thicken. The skin of stressed parts of palms and soles gradually became keratinized, thickened, black and chapped. Physical Examination:
- Nail examination: Fingernails and toenails partly thickened and raised in wedge shape, surface rough, opaque, and dirty brown
- Skin examination: Dry skin on trunk and extremities; punctate and flaky keratinized dark brown thickened patches on palms and soles
- Oral examination: Fissured tongue, oral leukokeratosis, and keratotic plaques in corners of mouth
- No natal teeth, follicular sebaceous cysts, hair loss, or other organ abnormalities
Diagnostic Tests:
- Genetic Testing:
  - Whole-exome sequencing performed
  - Sanger sequencing conducted for validation
  Test Result: Identified heterozygous c.1460–2_1460del variant in exon 9 of KRT6A gene (NM_005554.4)
  - Parental genetic testing showed absence of mutation in both parents
Final Diagnosis: Pachyonychia congenita type 6a (PC-k6a)

Case Information: A man in his 20s presented with a 6-year history of multiple growing papules superimposed on an asymptomatic firm plaque on the occipital scalp. At 3 years of age, scarlike macules appeared on the occipital scalp and coalesced to form a larger plaque with sparse hair. Simultaneously, multiple brown-red, dome-shaped papules developed on the nose and gradually evolved to involve the cheeks. Rubbery plaques also developed on the sacrococcygeal area, along with hypopigmented patches on the right buttock. The patient has no systemic symptoms or mental impairment and denied a family history of genetic disease.
Physical Examination: Physical examination revealed a group of unevenly distributed skin-colored-to-pink, discrete, coalescing papules superimposed on a firm plaque on the occipital scalp.
Diagnostic Tests: - Histopathology findings:  1. Biopsy from scalp papule showed:     - Dense fibrosis with thick collagen bundles throughout dermis     - Prominent decrease of hair follicles     - Fibroplasia arranged in whorled and onionskin pattern     - Capillary vessel proliferation with ectasia between collagen bundles     - Scattered fibroblasts     - Perifollicular fibrosis with distortion of hair follicles    2. Biopsy from facial papule showed:     - Features of angiofibroma  Images:  - Figure A: Clinical photograph showing firm plaque with superimposed papules and decreased hair density  - Figure B: Histology showing diffuse dense fibrosis  - Figure C: Histology (H&E) showing capillary vessel proliferation with ectasia  - Figure D: Histology showing perifollicular fibroblastic proliferation

Case Information: A 30-year-old woman presented with a large unhealed ulcer measuring 20 × 14-cm² that had persisted for the past 3 years. Diagnostic Tests: 1. Genetic Testing:- Blood cell genotyping showed compound heterozygous alterations in COL7A1- RNA transcript analysis using MinION platform:  * Affected skin epidermis: Dominant RNA transcripts with premature stop codons, wild-type reads rare (1.1%)  * Potentially revertant area epidermis: High rates of wild-type transcripts (35.0%)  * Revertant skin dermis: Wild-type reads rare (1.9%)2. Microscopy:- Immunofluorescence and transmission electron microscopy of the grafted area showed type VII collagen and anchoring fibrilsImages:- Figure 1A: Sanger sequencing showing compound heterozygous COL7A1 alteration- Figure 1B: Image of the persistent ulcer on patient's back- Figure 1C: Image of revertant skin patch on left forearm

Case Information: A man in his 20s presented with a 2-month history of itchy skin lesions on his trunk, arms, and groin. There are prominent vacuolar changes at the dermoepidermal junction, and in some areas mild lymphocytic exocytosis.    Additional findings: Dermal lymphocytes expressed CD3, CD4, and CD8 with slight predominance of CD4-positive over CD8-positive lymphocytes in the infiltrate with no immunophenotypic alterations.- Clinical Images:  Image Title: Clinical presentation  Image Description: Shows several erythematous annular patches on the flanks and periumbilical region.

Case Information: A 30-year-old woman presented with a 15-year history of recurrent ankle swelling and intermittent painful and tender nodules on her legs and feet. Physical Examination: Patient had large, extremely tender, irregularly shaped circumferential leg ulcers, with violaceous and purpuric borders, areas of eschar, and a fibrinous base. Reticulated erythema and purpura were present on hands. Faintly erythematous, blanching macules and papules coalescing into patches and thin plaques were observed on bilateral thighs. Genital and oral ulcers were also present.
Diagnostic Tests: - Laboratory findings: Elevated C-reactive protein, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, mildly elevated C3, leukocytosis, and hyperglycemia.- Imaging studies:  - Doppler ultrasound of lower extremities showed venous stasis  - Systemic imaging revealed splenomegaly and involvement of retroperitoneal, pelvic, and inguinal lymph nodes- Pathological findings:  - Skin biopsy (left thigh): Monomorphic, perivascular, and periadnexal atypical lymphocytic infiltrate  - Skin biopsy (purpuric edge of leg ulceration): Epidermal necrosis with numerous fibrin thrombi and intra-/perivascular atypical lymphocytic infiltrate showing monotony and hyperchromasia  - Immunohistochemical staining: CD3-positive T cells coexpressing CD4 and CD8 with preserved expression of CD7  - Flow cytometry: Phenotypically abnormal T-cell population with CD52 expression
Final Diagnosis: T-cell prolymphocytic leukemia (T-PLL)

Case Information: An 81-year-old man initially developed a cough, subjective fever, and shortness of breath. He was first treated with azithromycin for bronchitis, followed by levofloxacin after a right upper lobe infiltrate was found. His symptoms progressed despite antibiotics. Medical history includes coronary artery disease with coronary artery bypass (two years prior), atrial fibrillation on rivaroxaban, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, stage 3 chronic kidney disease, and type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c: 8.1%). He had spent time at a Northern Minnesota cabin but reported minimal outdoor exposure. Previous missionary work in Colombia ended two years before admission. Never smoked and rarely consumed alcohol.
Physical Examination: Vital signs: Temperature 38.6°C, Pulse 130 beats/min, Blood Pressure 114/61 mm Hg, Respiratory Rate 22 breaths/min, Oxygen Saturation 82% (improved to >90% with 5 L/min oxygen). Lung sounds were diminished in the right upper lobe, with diffuse crackles in the right lung base. No clubbing or cyanosis. Cardiac examination showed tachycardic irregularly irregular rhythm, systolic ejection murmur, and mild pitting edema to the knee bilaterally. No cervical or axillary lymphadenopathy. Neurologic and skin examinations were normal. Later developed right-sided ptosis and anisocoria with an asymmetrically miotic right pupil (more evident in dark environment).
Diagnostic Tests:
Laboratory Tests:
- Leukocytosis: 15,000/mm³
- Procalcitonin: 0.77 ng/mL
- Positive Blastomyces urine antigen
- Positive Histoplasmosis blood antigen
- Negative results for Mycobacterium tuberculosis
- Negative antigen tests for Cryptococcus, Legionella, and influenza
- Negative Paracoccidioides serum antibody

1. Chest Radiograph
Title: Initial Chest Radiograph
Description: Dense consolidation of the right upper lobe, patchy nodular infiltrates of left upper lobe, 5-mm noncalcified rounded infiltrate in posterolateral left lower lobe

2. CT and MRI
Title: Emergency Head CT and Brain MRI
Description: Both showed no acute process

Other Tests:
Bronchoscopy findings: Moderate clear secretions with erythematous and edematous bronchial mucosa
Bronchoalveolar lavage: Spherical broadly based budding yeast on Grocott-Gomori methenamine silver stain
Final Diagnosis: Horner syndrome secondary to invasive Blastomyces pneumonia

Case Information: A man in his late 60s with a 15-year history of Parkinson disease (Hoehn and Yahr score, 3) and 20-year history of sleep disturbance presented with worsening dream enactment behavior. While the patient's daytime motor symptoms improved following deep brain stimulation, episodes of dream enactment became progressively more violent, resulting in assault on his wife and self-injurious behaviors. Yelling occurred multiple times per night. Violent behaviors were reported every other night, such as punching through walls or striking furniture.
Physical Examination: Despite removing all objects from the bedroom and laying the mattress onto the floor, the patient continued to strike out at walls and the ground, causing self-injury and damage to property during sleep episodes. One severe episode resulted in a head laceration after striking the wall, requiring emergency department evaluation.
Diagnostic Tests: - Initial polysomnography demonstrated obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), with an Apnea Hypopnea Index score of 8.5.  - Emergency department neuroimaging was unremarkable.  - Repeat polysomnography demonstrated an Apnea Hypopnea Index score of 12.8.    Figure Title: Rapid Eye Movement Behavior Disorder and Violent Events
Final Diagnosis: Advanced Parkinson disease
Options: {'A': 'Neurodegenerative Disease Progression (e.g., Dementia with Lewy Bodies)', 'B': 'Advanced Parkinson disease', 'C': "Parkinson's Disease with Sleep Disturbance", 'D': 'REM Sleep Behavior Disorder (RBD)'}

Case Information: A 19-year-old female student (weight: 35 kg, height: 159 cm) was admitted with grade III dyspnea. She was prescribed diuretics (spironolactone–torsemide combination) and aspirin post-surgery. After starting a new medication at low dose (25 mg per day divided into two doses) for two days, the dose was increased to 25 mg twice daily on the third day. She was discharged on the fifth postoperative day but was readmitted the same evening.

Case Information: A patient in their 30s with obesity and a history of alcohol use was found to have recurrent arrhythmia during hospitalization. The patient had a prolonged hospital stay complicated by delirium tremens, intubation, and ventilator-associated pneumonia requiring multiple courses of antibiotics. Two weeks prior, the patient had undergone insertion of a peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC) and tracheostomy. The arrhythmia episodes were particularly noticeable when the patient assumed a left lateral decubitus position, associated with palpitations but without chest pain, dyspnea, presyncope or hemodynamic compromise.
Physical Examination: Two-dimensional echocardiography showed normal left ventricular function without structural abnormalities.
Diagnostic Tests: - Cardiac Monitoring:    Image Title: Cardiac Monitoring Rhythm Strip    Image Description: Shows fusion beat (hybrid complex from sinus and ventricular beat), and a premature ventricular complex similar in morphology to the wide QRS complex arrhythmia    - Chest Radiography:    Finding: Revealed displacement of the PICC tip into the right atrium at the level of the tricuspid valve
Final Diagnosis: Position-dependent ventricular tachycardia induced by displaced PICC line

Case Information: A man in his 80s presented with an 18-month history of skin changes. The patient complained about a mild tingling sensation within the lesion. Current medications included finasteride, montelukast, enalapril, and metoprolol. Medical history was significant for right total reverse shoulder replacement 5 years prior.
Physical Examination: An irregularly linear, rusty-brown, atrophic, streaklike plaque extending from the tip of his right shoulder to the right biceps, where it ended in arborizing purple plaque with scattered, slightly indurated, irregular papules within it. There was no warmth, thrills, or pulsations across the affected area. Apart from the described lesion, physical examination was unremarkable, and he had full range of motion of his right shoulder.

Final Diagnosis: Intralymphatic histiocytosis

Case Information: A man in his 40s presented with a 12-month history of nasal swelling. The swelling had gradually increased in size since onset, causing symptoms of pain and right-sided nasal blockage. No associated discharge from the swelling was reported. The patient did not report rhinorrhea or anosmia. The nasal swelling affected the patient psychosocially, causing emotional distress due to its prominent appearance, leading him to become housebound and experience social isolation. Medical history included anxiety.
Physical Examination: Clinical examination revealed a prominent cystic swelling related to the external nose on the right side with an associated internal component. The cystic swelling was fluctuant, fluid filled, and tender. On anterior rhinoscopy, it was seen to be narrowing the internal nasal valve.
Diagnostic Tests: - Imaging studies:    Image Title: MRI    Image Description: MRI showed no association with intracranial structures.    - Histopathologic Analysis:    Image Title: Histopathologic Image    Image Description: Analysis showed a nasal cyst lined by double-layered epithelium with outer flat basal cells and inner eosinophilic cells. Hematoxylin-eosin stain under high power view showing thin squamous lining and focal surface corrugated cuticle (original magnification ×400).
Final Diagnosis: Intranasal steatocystoma simplex
Options: {'A': 'Epidermoid cyst', 'B': 'Intranasal steatocystoma simplex', 'C': 'Nasal epidermoid (sebaceous) cyst', 'D': 'Dermoid cyst'}

Case Information: A woman in her 70s with a medical history of atrial fibrillation, asthma, gastroesophageal reflux disease, Schatzki ring status after multiple dilations, pulmonary hypertension, and hypothyroidism presented with a more than 10-year history of dysphagia. The patient reported difficulty swallowing dry and heavy-consistency foods. She denied odynophagia, otalgia, hematemesis, or hemoptysis. In the past, she had multiple esophagogastroduodenoscopies with dilations for her Schatzki ring that provided some relief; however, they did not completely resolve her symptoms.
Physical Examination: Clinical and video swallow evaluation revealed hypertrophy of the cricopharyngeus muscle. During surgery, a palpable thick broad cricopharyngeus muscle was identified.
Diagnostic Tests: - Imaging Studies:    1. Preoperative esophagographic imaging       Image Title: Preoperative barium swallow imaging       Image Description: Showing obvious hypertrophy of the cricopharyngeal muscle. Thumbprint sign with cricopharyngeal bar was present on esophagram.        2. Postoperative imaging       Image Title: Postoperative day 1 barium swallow imaging       Image Description: Showing no retained cricopharyngeal bar as well as no esophageal leak.  - Pathological Examination:    Histopathologic examination revealed:    - Nonnecrotizing granulomas and mononuclear inflammatory infiltrates in the endomysium    - Adipose tissue deposition    - Endomysial fibrosis    - Increased variation in muscle fiber size    - Increased internal nuclei    - Rare necrotic fibers    Special stains for acid-fast, fungal, and bacterial microorganisms were negative.
Final Diagnosis: Granulomatous myositis of the cricopharyngeus muscle due to sarcoidosis

Case Information: A man in his 40s presented with a new pigmented lesion on his left second toenail. The lesion first appeared 12 months before presentation and was progressively growing. This was his first time presenting to a physician for this problem, and no prior treatments had been attempted. The lesion was asymptomatic. He was otherwise well and denied any fevers or weight loss.
Physical Examination: Clinical examination revealed a dark brown longitudinal pigment band covering the full length of the toenail of the left second digit with corresponding nail thickening over the pigmented band. The pigment was the same width throughout the band and extended to the proximal nail fold. No other nail or skin lesions were found at the time of examination.
Diagnostic Tests: - Histopathologic examination:    Image Title: Histopathologic examination with hematoxylin-eosin stain    Image Description: Specimens shown at original magnification of ×40, ×100, and ×200        Findings:    - Bland spindle cell proliferation within the nail matrix with invaginations of the epithelium    - No melanocytic lesions observed    - Immunostaining negative for S100 and CD34    - Intracorneal hemorrhage and adjacent verrucous hyperkeratosis present    - Villous fibroepithelial projections into the nail plate without melanocytic proliferation or keratinocyte atypia    Image Title: Clinical Photograph    Image Description: Dark brown longitudinal pigment band on the second toenail with pigment extending to the proximal nail fold
Final Diagnosis: Pigmented onychomatricoma
Options: {'A': 'Subungual melanoma', 'B': 'Longitudinal Melanonychia', 'C': 'Onychomatricoma', 'D': 'Pigmented onychomatricoma'}

Case Information: A 56-year-old woman presented with a medical history including hypermobility syndrome diagnosed in 1998 and cosmetic breast augmentation surgery in 2002. From 2014 onwards, she experienced recurrent abscesses, indurated erythematous plaques, and sclerosis on both upper legs. She showed no symptoms of autoinflammation or autoimmunity besides periodic fever.
Physical Examination: Clinical examination revealed:- Indurated erythematous plaques and sclerosis on both upper legs- Presence of abscesses on upper legs- No other significant physical findings noted
Diagnostic Tests: Laboratory Tests:- Antinuclear antibodies: negativeImaging Studies:- Magnetic resonance imaging (2019): revealed bilaterally ruptured breast implants- Axillary ultrasound: revealed extensive deposits in multiple axillary lymph nodesPathological Examinations:1. Skin and Muscle Biopsies (Upper legs and abdomen):   - Showed perivascular lymphoplasmacytic inflammation   - Extensive subcutaneous sclerosis of septa and fascia   - Granulomatous inflammation   - Modified oil-red-o (MORO) staining: revealed particles in macrophages with extensive histiocytic reaction and deep fibrosis2. Breast Capsule Pathology:   - Showed extensive accumulation of foamy macrophages3. Axillary Lymph Node Examination:   - Revealed extensive leakage and surrounding inflammation   - No signs of lymphomaImages Referenced:- Figure 1: Clinical images showing abscesses and sclerosis of upper legs- Figure 2: Histopathological images of breast capsule- Figure 3: Various histopathological images showing inflammatory reactions

Case Information: A 31-year-old male nursing student with a history of axillary hyperhidrosis presented with excessive sweating of the buttocks, most severe at the intergluteal cleft and sacrum. He had previously treated his axillary condition successfully with 20% aluminum chloride; however, treatment of the intergluteal cleft and sacrum with aluminum chloride was unsuccessful. Due to the severity of the condition, he used absorbent pads yet continued to soak through his pants and was consequently unable to attend work.
Physical Examination: The Minor starch-iodine test was performed to help localize the sites of overactive sweating. Areas of significant sweating were identified in the sacral region (10 × 3-cm area) and intergluteal cleft (6 × 8-cm area).
Diagnostic Tests: - Clinical Tests:    Test Name: Minor starch-iodine test    Purpose: To localize areas of excessive sweating    Results: Identified areas of significant sweating in the sacral and intergluteal regions    - Visual Documentation:    Image Title: Grid Markings on the Sacrum and Intergluteal Cleft    Image Description: A 10 × 3-cm grid was drawn on the sacrum and a 6 × 8-cm grid was drawn on the intergluteal cleft, with the outermost dots omitted
Final Diagnosis: Intergluteal and sacral hyperhidrosis
Options: {'A': 'Intergluteal and sacral hyperhidrosis', 'B': 'Primary focal hyperhidrosis (gluteal region)', 'C': 'Localized infection or intertrigo', 'D': 'Idiopathic localized hyperhidrosis'}

Case Information: A 67-year-old man with a history of mood disorder, Parkinson's disease, hyperlipidemia, and mild cognitive impairment presented after an unwitnessed fall at home. Family noticed increasing weakness, fatigue, and confusion a few weeks before presentation. Patient reported decreased appetite and mild dyspnea, especially on exertion. No significant weight loss, dysphagia, or odynophagia. No history of smoking.
Physical Examination: Patient was in no acute distress. Neurologic examination revealed resting tremors in bilateral hands but was otherwise nonfocal. Abdominal examination was unremarkable.
Diagnostic Tests: - Laboratory findings:  * Elevated serum creatinine: 3.7 mg/dL (327 μmol/L)  * Hemoglobin: 10.5 g/dL (105 g/L)  * α-fetoprotein and β-human chorionic gonadotropin levels: normal  - Imaging studies:  * CT head and C-spine: unremarkable  * CT chest: lobulated anterior mediastinal mass measuring 13.1 × 7.7 × 5.8 cm  * Contrast CT chest: showed lobar and segmental pulmonary emboli in right middle and lower lobes  * MRI brain with and without contrast: no intracranial mass lesion  - Biopsy results:  * Immunohistochemistry: Positive for P40 immunostain  * Negative for thyroid transcription factor-1 and napsin A immunostains  - Images:  * Figure 1 Title: Squamous cell carcinoma from a mediastinal mass  * Figure 2 Title: CT chest with IV contrast (axial view) showing anterior mediastinal mass and right-sided pulmonary embolism  * Figure 3 Title: CT chest with IV contrast (sagittal view) showing anterior mediastinal mass
Final Diagnosis: Secondary squamous cell carcinoma of the anterior mediastinum with unknown primary site

Case Information: A 71-year-old man presented with three weeks of jaundice, weight loss, abdominal pain, and dark-colored urine. He also reported bilateral lower-extremity weakness that was most noticeable when attempting to arise from a chair, resulting in several ground-level falls. He had no history of autoimmune or neuromuscular conditions. Despite his recent falls, he did not have any prolonged periods of being down that could contribute to muscle injury. He had been taking atorvastatin as a home medication.
Physical Examination: Physical findings included:- Jaundice and significant scleral icterus- Epigastric tenderness without guarding or rebound- Mild weakness in bilateral hip flexor muscles

His medications included metoprolol, amlodipine, and over-the-counter acetaminophen.
Physical Examination: Blood pressure 140/80 mm Hg, pulse irregular at 130 beats/min, respiratory rate and oxygen saturation normal. Jugular venous pressure estimated at 10 cm, heart sounds demonstrated variable intensity of S1 and S2, and no murmurs, gallops, or rubs were appreciated. The remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable.
Diagnostic Tests: - Laboratory tests: Hemoglobin declined from 14.4 g/L to 12.6 g/L over six weeks. Leukocyte count 14.4 × 10⁹/L, troponin 0.04 µg/L, brain natriuretic peptide 393 ng/L, INR 7.7. Other tests including platelets, electrolytes, glucose, creatinine, liver enzymes, lipase, partial thromboplastin time, and procalcitonin were within normal limits.- Imaging studies:  1. Electrocardiograph: Showed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular rate and low-voltage QRS in the limb leads  2. CT angiogram and non-contrast CT:     Image Title: Chest, abdomen, and pelvis CT     Image Description: High-density anterior pericardial effusion measuring approximately 3.8 cm in thickness, mild compression of the right lateral ventricle, and reflux of contrast material into the hepatic veins  3. Transthoracic echocardiogram:     Image Title: Two-dimensional echocardiogram (parasternal long-axis view)     Image Description: Shimmering echodensity in the anterior pericardial space consistent with a complex pericardial effusion with signs of tamponade physiology
Final Diagnosis: Warfarin-induced hemopericardium

Case Information: A 28-year-old woman presented with pustules beneath the fingernails accompanied by redness and swelling of distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints in both hands. She received successive diagnoses and was initially treated with oral cefuroxime, followed by a combination of retinoids with topical corticosteroids, and oral methotrexate with topical corticosteroids. Despite therapy, her pustules and joint swelling worsened.
Physical Examination: Physical examination revealed 5 erythematous swollen and tender DIP joints that were accompanied by pustules under dystrophic fingernails in both hands.
Diagnostic Tests: - Laboratory tests: Screening assay for interferon-γ release for tuberculosis and hepatitis B and C were negative. Bacterial culture, complete blood cell count and coagulation tests had negative results.- Imaging studies:  Image Title: Hand radiography before treatment  Image Description: Bone erosions in the heads of phalanges in the middle and ring fingers on the left hand. No bone erosion in the fingers on the right hand.
Final Diagnosis: Acrodermatitis continua of Hallopeau (ACH)
Options: {'A': 'Acrodermatitis continua of Hallopeau (ACH)', 'B': 'Psoriatic arthritis with nail involvement', 'C': 'Psoriatic arthritis (PsA)', 'D': 'Pustular psoriasis'}

Case Information: A man in his 40s presented after developing multiple skin lesions. He had been diagnosed with metastatic lung adenocarcinoma the previous year and received 2 courses of radiotherapy to his head and leg and 4 cycles of carboplatin, pemetrexed, and bevacizumab followed by nivolumab (3 mg/kg) administered every 2 weeks. After 8 cycles of nivolumab over 5 months, he developed 1- to 2-mm shiny papules scattered on the upper limbs. Similar lesions spread to the rest of his body over the subsequent 3 months. He subsequently developed pruritus.
Physical Examination: Multiple 1- to 2-mm shiny papules scattered on the upper limbs and body. The lesions on the untreated arm coalesced into plaques.
Diagnostic Tests: - Skin biopsy findings:  - Focal lymphohistiocytic infiltrates beneath a thinned epidermis  - Circumscribed by elongated rete ridges  - Vacuoles in the dermoepidermal junction  - Melanin incontinence  Images:  Figure 1. Clinical Presentation Before and After Treatment With Topical Steroids  Description: A) Numerous 1- to 2-millimeter skin-colored shiny papules on the upper back. B) Comparison of treated right forearm versus untreated left forearm.  Figure 2. Histological Examination  Description: Skin biopsy specimen from upper limbs showing discrete foci of lymphocytes and histiocytes immediately beneath the epidermis, surrounded by elongated rete ridges (hematoxylin-eosin, original magnification ×10).
Final Diagnosis: Generalized Lichen nitidus
Options: {'A': 'Generalized Lichen nitidus', 'B': 'Lichen planus', 'C': 'Subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus', 'D': 'Nivolumab-induced lichenoid dermatitis'}

Case Information: A 19-year-old white female college student presented with a one-week history of occipital headache and a one-month history of intermittent right calf pain, generalized malaise, and fevers. Her medical and dental history were unremarkable, and she had no history of alcohol or illicit drug use.
Physical Examination: Temperature of 39°C, diastolic murmur in the left parasternal area, and a pansystolic murmur at the apex.
Diagnostic Tests:
- Laboratory Tests:
  * Mild anemia (102 g/L; normal 120–160 g/L)
  * Raised C-reactive protein (1394 nmol/L; <95 nmol/L)
  * Other blood tests were within the normal range
  * Blood cultures grew a Gram-positive streptococcus

- Imaging Studies:
  * Brain MRI: small left superior frontal gyrus cortical infarct
  * Transoesophageal echocardiogram:
    - Title: Transgastric long-axis view
    - Description: 3 cm vegetation prolapsing across a trileaflet aortic valve, severe aortic regurgitation, moderate mitral regurgitation with perforation of the anterior mitral leaflet
  * Duplex ultrasound and CT angiogram:
    - Title: Right lower limb arteries with color flow Doppler
    - Description: Right peroneal mycotic aneurysm (20 × 19 × 17 mm), occluded right brachial artery with adequate collateral circulation

- Pathological Findings:
  * Aortic valve histopathology: white cells with Gram-positive cocci
  * Valve culture: negative
Final Diagnosis: Abiotrophia defectivus infectious endocarditis
Options: {'A': 'Bacterial endocarditis with septic emboli', 'B': 'Abiotrophia defectivus infectious endocarditis', 'C': 'Infective Endocarditis', 'D': 'Infective endocarditis caused by Streptococcus viridans'}

Case Information: A 54-year-old man with a medical history of hypertension and squamous cell carcinoma of the subglottis (treated with cisplatin and radiation therapy 8 months prior) presented to the emergency department with abdominal pain and shortness of breath. The patient rapidly deteriorated, requiring intubation and multiple vasopressors. His hospital course lasted 2 months and was complicated by pulmonary embolism, atrial fibrillation, and gastrointestinal bleeding.
Physical Examination: The initial presentation showed signs of respiratory distress. The patient's condition rapidly deteriorated requiring intubation and hemodynamic support with multiple vasopressors including norepinephrine, phenylephrine, and vasopressin.
Diagnostic Tests:
- Laboratory Tests:
  * Multiple blood cultures, pleural fluid culture, and urine cultures showed no organism growth
  * Serum calcium levels were within normal range (2.33–2.40 mmol/L [9.3–9.6 mg/dL])
  * Baseline estimated glomerular filtration rate >60, serum creatinine = 65.58 micromoles/L (0.86 mg/dL)

- Imaging Studies:
  * Initial CT: Right-sided pneumonia with empyema
  * Serial CT scans: Development of diffuse calcification of the LV myocardium over 6 days
  * Coronary angiography: Demonstrated nonobstructive coronary artery disease
  * Cardiac MRI: Revealed dilated LV with severely reduced ejection fraction and extensive subepicardial late gadolinium enhancement
  * PET scan findings: Initial normal myocardial FDG uptake, followed by reduced uptake, and later normalized FDG uptake

- Image Descriptions:
  * Figure 1: CT chest showing diffuse, thick, and linear LV calcification 6 days after admission
  * Figure 2: Cardiac MRI showing dilated LV with reduced systolic function
  * Figure 3: Serial CT scans showing calcification progression and reduction over 3 years
  * Figure 4: Serial PET scans showing FDG uptake changes
Final Diagnosis: Reversible left ventricular calcification postsepsis
Options: {'A': 'Severe sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy', 'B': 'Dystrophic Myocardial Calcification', 'C': 'Reversible left ventricular calcification postsepsis', 'D': 'Sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy with dystrophic calcification'}

Case Information: A 51-year-old man presented with a 6-year history of chronic cough. The cough was productive (egg cup full of sputum daily), present day and night, and associated with numerous triggers. He denied haemoptysis or breathlessness, and his weight was stable. The cough was rated 9/10 in severity and occurred largely in bouts, often associated with retching and dizziness. It had worsened in severity in the 2 years prior. Past medical history included a 15-year history of ulcerative colitis, initially managed medically, with a subsequent total colectomy (2017) and re-anastomosis (2018). He was an ex-smoker, having quit in 1999, with a 5 pack-years smoking history. He worked in construction, with occasional exposure to spray paints.
Physical Examination: Body mass index was 25 kg·m−2, no clubbing present, and chest examination was unremarkable.
Diagnostic Tests: - Spirometry Results:    FEV1: 3.89 L (111% predicted)    FVC: 5.74 L (131% predicted)    FEV1/FVC: 0.67    TLCO: 113%    KCO: 101%  - Blood Tests:    Haemoglobin: 150 g·L−1    WBC: 5.6 ×10⁹ cells·L−1    Neutrophils: 3.90 ×10⁹ cells·L−1    Eosinophils: 0.20 ×10⁹ cells·L−1    CRP: 3 mg·L−1    Myeloperoxidase: 1.3 AI    Proteinase 3 Ab: >0.2 AI    Total IgE: 95.3 kU·L−1    IgA, IgM, IgG: Normal  - Bronchoscopy Findings:    Description: Marked endobronchial inflammation with purulent sputum in both lower lobes    BAL cell differential:      - Macrophages: 56%      - Neutrophils: 29%      - Lymphocytes: 12%      - Eosinophils: 0%      - Others: 3%    Bronchial wash: Low numbers of oral commensals only  - Imaging:    Title: High-resolution computed tomography of the thorax    Description: Shows mild lower lobe bronchiectasis, more marked on the right side. Airways appear abnormally widened and thickened with signet ring sign. No emphysema, ground-glass changes, or cavitation.
Final Diagnosis: IBD-associated airways disease and inflammatory bronchiectasis
Options: {'A': 'Bronchiectasis', 'B': 'Non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis', 'C': 'IBD-associated airways disease and inflammatory bronchiectasis', 'D': 'Inflammatory bowel disease-associated lung disease'}

Case Information: A 13-year-old boy presented with a five-day history of left scrotal soreness and oedema. The patient had no lower urinary tract symptoms and no major relevant medical or surgical history. Based on penile characteristics, the patient was diagnosed with occult penis. Other congenital anomalies of the genitourinary tract were excluded based on imaging findings.
Physical Examination: Scrotal investigation revealed an enlarged testis, epididymis, and infected appendages. No torsion was evident in the sperm vessels; however, purulent foci were visible on the surface of the vessels, and their texture was tough and inseparable. The patient was at Tanner stage G4P4.
Diagnostic Tests: - Laboratory tests: Blood workup showed mild leukocytosis (18.5 × 109/L). Urine analysis was negative. Pus culture isolated P. aeruginosa, susceptible to ceftazidime and ofloxacin.- Imaging studies:  1. Doppler ultrasonography: Revealed an enlarged left testis with heterogeneous echo pattern, enhanced vascularity, and retained testicular blood flow.  2. Follow-up sonography: Showed heterogeneous echotexture in the left testis and epididymis, with a mild whirlpool sign and hyperechogenicity in the inguinal area.  3. MRI Image (at 60 days):     Title: MR imaging of testes     Description: Left testis showed no enhancement in T2, indicating disappearance of the left testis.- Pathological examination: Showed hemorrhagic infarction of the left testicular appendage and fibrocystic-like tissue with inflammatory cell infiltration.

Case Information: A 66-year-old female patient with a history of Hashimoto's thyroiditis and thrombocytopenia. The patient had consistently shown leukocytopenia and thrombocytopenia in routine blood tests over the years. Initial blood test revealed a leukocyte count of 3.16 × 10^9/L and a platelet count of 109.00 × 10^9/L. No other symptoms were present, and no significant abnormalities were detected in cardiac enzymes or blood coagulation. Four hours post-procedure, the patient experienced intermittent abdominal pain and occasional nausea. Nine hours following the operation, the patient experienced recurrent right abdomen pain progressing to involve the entire abdomen, with persistent nausea and perceived increase in body temperature.
Physical Examination: Initial examination showed tenderness in the right abdomen, without rebound tenderness and muscular tension, with normal bowel sounds. Later examination revealed diffuse tenderness with rebound tenderness, primarily in the right abdomen. Dullness to percussion was observed, while pain during percussion in the liver area was absent. Shifting dullness to percussion was negative, and normal bowel sounds were detected. Maximum body temperature was 37.8°C.
Diagnostic Tests:
- Laboratory Tests:
  - WBC count: 3.43 × 10^9/L
  - Neutrophil percentage: 85.40%
  - Lymphocyte percentage: 9.40%
  - Absolute lymphocytes: 0.32 × 10^9/L
  - Platelet count: 79.00 × 10^9/L
  - CRP level: 2.45 mg/L
  - Procalcitonin level: 0.58 ng/ml

- Imaging Studies:
  CT Scan Results: Edematous thickening of the intestinal wall in the ascending colon with peripheral exudative changes. No signs of intestinal perforation or obstruction.
  Follow-up CT: Distension of anterior wall of ascending colon with gas, exudation in subserosa of middle section, and small quantity of ascites.

- Bacterial Culture:
  Ascites culture yielded growth of Escherichia coli.

- Histological Examination:
  Showed edema in all layers of intestinal wall, predominant thickening and edema in submucosa, diffuse infiltration of neutrophils with presence of abscesses. Bacterial colonies observed in submucosa surrounding the ESD lesion.
Final Diagnosis: Phlegmonous enteritis
Options: {'A': 'Ischemic colitis', 'B': 'Neutropenic enterocolitis (typhlitis)', 'C': 'Infectious colitis', 'D': 'Phlegmonous enteritis'}

Case Information: A 52-year-old woman with a history of Sjogren's syndrome initially presented to a regional hospital complaining of sudden chest tightness. On day 3, the patient developed severe respiratory distress and experienced cardiac arrest characterized by severe ventricular arrhythmias refractory to conventional cardiopulmonary resuscitation efforts. Following tracheal intubation, she underwent peripheral venous-arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (V-A ECMO) as a rescue therapy, accompanied by intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) implantation. On day 4, due to worsening cardiovascular status and persistent electrical instability despite V-A ECMO and IABP, the patient was transferred to another hospital by helicopter.
Physical Examination: Upon arrival at the hospital, the patient's blood pressure was 83/64 mmHg while receiving mechanical circulatory support with ECMO (operating at a flow rate of 3.5 L/min) and IABP. Bedside transthoracic echocardiography showed that the global wall motion was severely depressed, with an estimated left ventricular ejection fraction(LVEF) of approximately 10%.
Diagnostic Tests:
- Laboratory Tests:
  - Platelet count: 14 × 10^9/L
  - Troponin T: 14210 ng/L (ref: 0-14 ng/L)
  - NT-proBNP: 8296 pg/mL (ref: 0-125 pg/mL)
  - ALT: 116 IU/L (ref: 0-33 IU/L)
  - Creatinine: 134 μmol/L (ref: 45-84 umol/L)
  - Arterial blood gases: pH 7.25, lactate 4.87 mmol/L (ref: 0.5-2.2 mmol/L)

- Imaging Studies:
  - ECG: Revealed ventricular tachycardia
  - Chest X-ray: Showed pulmonary edema
  - Cardiac catheterization: Revealed normal coronary arteries

- Pathological Examination:
  - Histological analysis of left ventricular apex specimen showed myocardial necrosis, interstitial edema, and fibrosis, accompanied by infiltration of diffuse monocytes and scattered multinucleated giant cells
Final Diagnosis: Fulminant giant cell myocarditis
Options: {'A': 'Fulminant giant cell myocarditis', 'B': 'Fulminant myocarditis', 'C': 'Fulminant lymphocytic (viral) myocarditis', 'D': 'Giant cell myocarditis'}

Case Information: A healthy, immunocompetent 30-year-old man undergoing basic military training presented with a 4-day history of a progressive vesiculopustular eruption of the anterior neck and chin. The patient denied symptoms of pruritus, burning, or fever at time of presentation. He had not received the smallpox vaccination but had engaged in simulated unarmed combat with individuals who had recently been vaccinated. Medical history was noncontributory, and the patient was not taking any medications. During hospitalization, the patient developed fevers to 39°C, progressive edema of the face, and anterior cervical lymphadenopathy.
Physical Examination: More than 2 dozen monomorphic, broad-based folliculocentric pustules with central umbilication, overlying serum crust, and surrounding erythema and edema of the anterior neck, chin, and inferior cheeks.
Diagnostic Tests: - Histopathologic examination (Punch biopsy):     - Demonstrated viral changes to the epidermis with ballooning degeneration of keratinocytes with intracytoplasmic inclusions (Guarnieri bodies) and a brisk lymphocytic and neutrophilic infiltrate    - PCR Test:    - Confirmed presence of vaccinia virus      - Electron Microscopy:    - Demonstrated viral particles within the Guarnieri bodies      - Images:    Figure 1 Title: Clinical Findings    Description: Vesiculopustules with central umbilication in the beard area at time of presentation        Figure 2 Title: Histopathologic Findings From Lesional Skin of the Right Cheek    Description: Shows epidermal necrosis, intraepidermal edema, ballooning degeneration, necrotic keratinocytes, neutrophilic infiltrate, and Guarnieri bodies within keratinocytes
Final Diagnosis: Sycosis vaccinatum

Case Information: A male patient with history of primary radiotherapy for prostate cancer. The patient developed rising PSA levels despite androgen deprivation therapy (ADT). Age at presentation: 70 years. PSA level: 6 ng/mL. PSA doubling time: 6.6 months. ECOG performance status: <2.
